data.title: Novocure Shares Climb After Company Posts Higher 1Q Revenue, Raises Full-Year Outlook
data.description:
By Christopher Kuo

Novocure shares soared after the company posted double-digit first-quarter revenue growth and raised its full-year revenue outlook.

Shares of the oncology company climbed 27% on Thursday to $15.20. The company's shares are up 17% year to date.

The company raised its full-year revenue guidance. It now expects revenue of $690 million to $710 million. Analysts polled by FactSet are expecting full-year revenue of $698.9 million.

The company reported a loss of $71.1 million, or 62 cents a share, compared with a loss of $24.3 million, or 31 cents a share a year earlier. Analysts expected a loss per share of 51 cents.

Revenue rose 12% to $174.1 million, driven by active patient growth in European markets. Analysts expected revenue of $167.8 million.

Write to Christopher Kuo at chris.kuo@wsj.com

data.title: Mexico's Pemex Posts First Quarter Net Loss
data.description:
By Anthony Harrup

MEXICO CITY--Mexican state oil company Petroleos Mexicanos reported a wider net loss for the first quarter as sales and operating profit fell, while the highly leveraged concern continued to lower its debt.

Pemex posted a loss of 45.99 billion Mexican pesos ($2.62 billion) for the January-March quarter, compared with a loss of MXN43.33 billion in the year-earlier period. The company attributed the loss to lower sales, higher impairment of fixed assets, higher derivative-related costs and a foreign exchange loss.

First-quarter revenue fell 7.6% to MXN365.7 billion, with lower export sales due to a decline in export volume partially offset by greater domestic sales of gasoline, diesel and jet fuel. Operating profit fell 38% to MXN39.51 billion.

Production of crude oil and condensates rose 2.3% to 1.65 million barrels a day, and natural gas production grew 10% to 4.85 billion cubic feet a day. Pemex refineries processed 1.14 million barrels a day of crude oil, up from 933,000 barrels a day in the year-earlier quarter, with production of petroleum products up 22% at 1.11 million barrels a day.

Pemex's financial debt fell to $79 billion from $85.2 billion at the end of 2025, reaching its lowest level since 2014.

Write to Anthony Harrup at anthony.harrup@wsj.com

data.title: Carvana Earnings Boosted by 40% Jump in Vehicle Sales. The Stock Falls. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kit Norton and Al Root

Amid rising oil and new-car prices, the used-car business is doing just fine.

Carvana, the used-car retailer, posted better-than-expected first-quarter earnings and revenue on strong vehicle sales. The company, however, continues to focus on boosting profit margins.

The car dealer reported adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization of $672 million for the first quarter, topping analysts' consensus call for $646 million. Profit came in at $1.69 a share, up from $1.51 a year ago.

Revenue rose 52% to $6.43 billion, above Wall Street's forecast of $6.12 billion, according to FactSet.

Carvana stock was up more than 6% in premarket trading but the gains faded, leaving shares at $387.27 in early trading, down 2.2%. The S&P 500 was up 0.1% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average was up 1.1%.

Carvana stock has been on a wild ride lately, making calling the reaction to earnings difficult. Coming into Thursday, shares had gained 36% over the past month but remain down 6% this year.

Carvana sold 187,393 vehicles at retail in the period, up 40% from a year ago.

"Carvana had a solid quarter from a topline perspective," wrote Gordon Haskett analyst Robert Mollins in a note Thursday. "Looking out from here, we believe management will continue to execute well and should be able to sustain topline growth well above publicly traded dealership peers over the next few years."

The adjusted Ebitda margin was 10.4%, down from 11.5%. Wall Street predicted sales of 181,839 vehicles at retail for the quarter. Carvana is trying to maintain a rapid pace of growth while also expanding profit margins — a difficult accomplishment for any company.

The company reported total gross profit of $6,783 per unit in the first quarter, just a few dollars short of Wall Street's estimates but $155 lower than the $6,938 per unit a year ago.

Higher vehicle reconditioning costs, along with a drop in shipping revenue, hit retail gross profit per unit. A decline in wholesale gross profit and lower rates for customers also impacted profit margins per unit, according to the company.

"Reconditioning costs were higher year over year in Q1, but the team is making progress on centralizing planning/decision-making, building better tools/leveraging AI and strengthening training/workhouse development," wrote Wells Fargo analyst David Lantz in a research note.

Looking ahead, Carvana said it expects a sequential increase in both retail units sold and adjusted Ebitda in the second quarter. The used-car seller added it is on track to deliver "significant growth" in both vehicles sold at retail and adjusted Ebitda for the full year.

Carvana also once more reiterated its goal to sell 3 million vehicles at retail a year at a 13.5% adjusted Ebitda margin by some point between 2030 and 2035.

In March, Carvana's board approved a 5-for-1 stock split that will make shares easier for individual investors and the company's employees to purchase. Overall, stockholders have had quite the ride since the company went public for $15 a share in 2017. Shares soared to more than $300 each in 2021 at the height of the pandemic-era car-buying boom before slumping to as low as $5 by the end of 2022.

Before the earnings release on Wednesday, Morgan Stanley noted several headwinds facing the company, including inflation, interest rates, labor market concerns, and rising fuel costs.

The company managed through the headwinds, which is a relief to investors.

Write to Kit Norton at kit.norton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: T. Rowe Price Group 1Q 2026: Revenue $1.86B, EPS $2.23— 10-Q Summary
data.description:
T. Rowe Price Group reported first-quarter 2026 results with revenue of $1.86B and diluted EPS of $2.23, reflecting modest year‑over‑year growth as assets under management rose and the firm continued share repurchases and dividend increases.

Financial Highlights

MetricCurrent quarterPrior year quarterYoY change
Revenue¹$1.86B$1.76B5.3%
Net income²$498.2M$490.5M1.6%
Diluted EPS³$2.23$2.153.7%

¹ Reported as “Net revenues”. ² Reported as “Net income attributable to T. Rowe Price Group, Inc.”. ³ Reported as “Earnings per share on common stock of T. Rowe Price Group, Inc.”.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue growth was driven by a 9.6% rise in average assets under management and higher advisory fees.
  • Flows and transfers favored lower‑fee products, reducing the effective fee rate by about 1.6 basis points.
  • Performance and ratings momentum: 48.6% of rated U.S. funds earned 4- or 5-star ratings, with AUM‑weighted outperformance in fixed income and multi‑asset strategies.
  • Management pursued expense realignment, including restructuring charges, headcount reductions, and targeted technology investments to improve efficiency.
  • Liquidity allowed $340M in share repurchases and a higher dividend while the firm invested about $270M in technology capital expenditures for 2026.

Original SEC Filing: PRICE T ROWE GROUP INC [ TROW ] - 10-Q - Apr. 30, 2026

data.title: SERVICE CORP INTERNATIONAL 1Q 2026: Revenue $1.1B, EPS $0.97— 10-Q Summary
data.description:
SERVICE CORP INTERNATIONAL reported first-quarter 2026 results with revenue of $1.1B, modestly higher than $1.07B in the prior-year quarter, while diluted EPS was $0.97 versus $0.98 a year earlier and net income attributable to common stockholders declined to $135.81M from $142.88M.

Financial Highlights

MetricCurrent quarterPrior year quarterYoY change
Revenue¹$1.1B$1.07B2.1%
Net income²$135.81M$142.88M(4.9%)
Diluted EPS³$0.97$0.98(1%)

¹ Reported as “Total revenue”. ² Reported as “Net income attributable to common stockholders”. ³ Reported as “Diluted earnings per share”.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue mix shifted: comparable funeral revenue fell about 2.7% driven by fewer services, while cemetery revenue grew roughly 7.2%, led by property and merchandise sales.
  • Preneed insurance-funded production rose to $214.0M and preneed backlog reached $8.84B, reducing reliance on trust-funded sales.
  • Dignity Memorial served about 700,000 families annually; cremation rate increased to 64.5% and average funeral revenue per service grew 3.4%.
  • Company continued investments in new locations, acquisitions and headquarters construction, driving higher capital spending and acquisition outlays.
  • Preneed trust portfolios remained diversified (approximately 59% equity, 27% fixed income, 10% alternatives) with trust income supporting preneed revenue recognition.

data.title: CNX Resources Corp 1Q 2026: Revenue $786.65M, EPS $2.18— 10-Q Summary
data.description:
CNX Resources Corp reported first-quarter 2026 results with revenue of $786.65M and diluted EPS of $2.18, a sharp improvement versus the prior-year quarter driven by higher realized gas prices and volume growth.

Financial Highlights

MetricCurrent quarterPrior year quarterYoY change
Revenue¹$786.65M$82.39M854.8%
Net income²$348.15M($197.72M)276.1%
Diluted EPS³$2.18($1.34)262.7%

¹ Reported as “Total Revenue and Other Operating Income”. ² Reported as “Net Income (Loss)”. ³ Reported as “Earnings (Loss) per Share”.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue momentum was driven by higher realized gas prices and a 4.6 Bcfe increase in volumes versus the prior year.
  • Total company production rose to 152.4 Bcfe for the quarter, with shale volumes boosted by 3.8 Bcf from new wells and APEX-related additions.
  • Operational efficiency improved: per-unit LOE declined due to increased water reuse and reduced disposal, while transportation and DD&A per Mcfe rose modestly with higher utilization.
  • A substantial hedge program (115.1 Bcf for Q2; 459.2 Bcf for 2026) materially affected cash settlements and helped stabilize price exposure.
  • Midstream and commercial results included higher excess firm transportation income and stable third‑party gathering revenue; lower environmental attribute sales reduced other operating income.

Original SEC Filing: CNX Resources Corp [ CNX ] - 10-Q - Apr. 30, 2026

data.title: Big Tech Is on an AI Spending Spree. Our Stock Pick Sees Fatter Profit Ahead. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kit Norton

Shares of Quanta Services were tracking toward a record closing high on Thursday after the artificial intelligence infrastructure builder beat earnings estimates and raised its full-year outlook.

Just as big tech companies such as Meta Platforms raise their AI capital expenditure forecasts, Quanta Services is hiking its 2026 outlook, expecting full-year profit between $13.55 and $14.25 a share with revenue ranging from $34.7 billion to $35.2 billion.

Quanta Services in February forecast 2026 revenue of $33.25 billion to $33.75 billion with earnings of $12.65 to $13.35 a share. The current Wall Street consensus forecast is for full-year earnings of $13.08 a share on sales of $33.42 billion, according to FactSet.

Quanta Services is a heavy-construction company that builds electric plants and provides other services for the energy grid. Analysts see the company as a key beneficiary of the buildout of AI computing capacity, which requires massive amounts of electricity.

Quanta Services stock advanced 13% to $707.20 on Thursday, set for a record closing price. Shares are up 29% in April, part of a 67% advance so far this year. Over the past 12 months, the stock has rallied 123%.

Barron's made Quanta Services a top stock pick on Oct. 15, 2025. Since then, Quanta Services stock has gained more than 60%, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

For the first quarter, Quanta Services posted earnings of $2.68 a share, compared with $1.78 a share last year and well above Wall Street's $2.04 a share expectation. First-quarter revenue rose 26% to $7.87 billion, coming in above analysts consensus view of $7 billion.

Quanta Services also ended the first quarter with a record backlog of $48.5 billion.

CEO Duke Austin said on the conference call Thursday that utilities are being asked to double in size and that technology customers are demanding speed at scale. That's all good news for Quanta Services.

Austin highlighted that the company is investing $500 million to $700 million over the next several years in its power transformer manufacturing facilities and vertical supply chain strategy, which will double its power transformer manufacturing capacity.

Quanta Services also plans to nearly double its off-site manufacturing, fabrication, and logistics facilities over the next several years.

"We are experiencing significant demand for these services, particularly for data centers, and these programs are just a couple of examples of Quanta's ability to provide total solutions across converging markets that are designed to deliver speed and certainty," Austin said.

Write to Kit Norton at kit.norton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: South Sudan says it won't renew Oranto Petroleum's Block B3 licence
data.description:
South Sudan's government said on Thursday that it had decided not to renew Nigerian company Oranto Petroleum's Exploration and Production Sharing Agreement for Block B3.

  • In a statement, the East African country's petroleum ministry said a review of Oranto's performance over the six-year contract period found it had not met obligations like the completion of required seismic surveys and drilling commitments.

  • Oranto did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

  • The South Sudanese ministry said it had concluded that the non-renewal of the Block B3 licence is "in the best interest of the country".

  • "Block B3 is now open for new applications, and the Ministry of Petroleum welcomes interest from ... qualified international and regional oil and gas companies," its statement added.

  • The oil sector is one of South Sudan's main sources of revenue.

data.title: WTW shares fall by 12% after revision to Risk & Broking organic growth guidance
data.description:
By Scott Vincent

WTW’s share price fell by more than 12% in morning trading on Thursday after the broker announced lower than expected organic growth earlier in the day.

As at 12:15 p.m. ET, the broker's share price was down 12.7% at $253.37, despite adjusted earnings per share of $3.72 beating analysts' consensus of $3.66.

WTW reported 3% organic growth for the first quarter of 2026, with organic growth in its Risk & Broking segment falling to 2% from 7% in Q1 2025.

This represented a miss against consensus estimates of 4.8% at group level and 5.4% for Risk & Broking.

WTW disclosed on Thursday that it now expects mid-single digit full-year organic growth for the Risk & Broking segment, compared with previous guidance of mid-to-high single digit.

During the group’s earnings call, chief financial officer Andrew Krasner said Corporate Risk & Broking (CRB), which sits within the Risk & Broking segment, had tracked towards the lower end of its planning range during the quarter.

CRB reported organic growth of 2% during the first three months of the year, reducing to 1% when the impact of business settlement gains and fiduciary investment income are excluded.

Krasner said CRB had seen a miss in its new business target, and was also impacted to a lesser extent by a more competitive pricing environment.

However, he stressed that the first quarter does not define CRB’s trajectory for the rest of 2026.

“Given the strength and visibility of our pipeline, we remain confident in our ability to drive profitable growth for the full year. However, reflecting the slower start to the year, we are narrowing our full-year outlook for CRB organic growth to mid-single digits,” Krasner said.

WTW management also highlighted that the first quarter of 2025 had seen strong comparable organic growth of 9% in CRB, with this year’s Q1 also impacted by the timing of some new business activity.

Lucy Clarke, president of Risk & Broking, said WTW had expected the first quarter to be the lowest growth quarter of the year for CRB, largely due to the strength of Q1 2025.

She said North America had the best performance with international trailing other geographies, in part due to new business exposure to geopolitical headwinds.

“To a lesser extent, our growth was also impacted by an even more competitive pricing environment than we expected, particularly in the large and complex segment,” she said.

“Although we have returned to normalized growth in April, given the slower start to the year and those ongoing price headwinds, we are taking a prudent approach and narrowing our full year outlook to mid-single digits.”

data.title: Chicago grain futures fall on oil retreat, improved US weather outlook
data.description:
Key points:
  • Soy complex choppy as oil prices turn lower
  • Improved US weather forecasts ease planting concerns
  • Funds have been net sellers of corn, wheat and soymeal

By P.J. Huffstutter

Chicago Board of Trade grain futures reversed course on Thursday, following a retreat in crude oil prices, amid a flurry of technical trading and forecasts of improving weather across the central U.S., market analysts said.

The CBOT's most-active wheat contract was down 2.41% at $6.37-1/4 a bushel as of 10:56 a.m. CDT (1556 GMT), after hitting its highest price since June 2024 the previous session.

Pressure from the wheat market spilled over to corn futures, where the most-active contract dipped 0.94% to $4.73-1/4 a bushel, after rallying to a near one-year high on Wednesday.

Meanwhile, trading in soybean futures was choppy, with many contracts trading either side of unchanged early in the session.

The most-active soybean contract on the Chicago Board of Trade was down 0.1% at $11.95-3/4 a bushel after hitting its highest point in more than six weeks.

Global oil prices jumped to a four-year high of more than $126 a barrel on Thursday on concerns that the Iran war could worsen and lead to protracted Gulf supply disruption that could hurt global economic growth, but later retreated.

The strength of oil prices, which have risen on supply disruption linked to the Iran war, has buoyed the soy complex and corn futures, as soybeans and corn are feedstocks for biofuel.

Early planting of U.S. soybeans and corn has progressed well, and forecasts for drier weather for the U.S. Corn Belt through this weekend eased some market concerns that a spate of storms in the Midwest could delay seeding in some areas, market analysts said.

"To see some level of consolidation is not surprising," said Angie Setzer, partner at advisory firm Consus Ag Consulting. "Cooler heads are prevailing with the weather, too."

Export demand also remained relatively steady. The U.S. Department of Agriculture reported net export sales of old-crop U.S. wheat in the week ended April 23 at 226,100 metric tons and new-crop sales of 156,700 tons, both in line with trade expectations.

The USDA also reported net export sales of old-crop U.S. corn in the week ended April 23 at 1,597,800 tons, in line with expectations for 1,000,000 to 1,900,000 tons.

And USDA reported net export sales of old-crop U.S. soybeans in the week ended April 23 at 258,100 metric tons, toward the low end of trade expectations for 200,000 to 600,000 tons.

Commodity funds were net sellers of corn, wheat and soymeal, traders said on Wednesday. (CBOT/FUNDS)

data.title: Meta's AI Spending Seen Driving Revenue Growth — Market Talk
data.description: Meta Platforms' capital expenditures are likely to keep growing next year following a lift to CapEx guidance in the company's most recent quarter, KeyBanc Capital Markets analysts write in a note. But though the market reaction points to disapproval of the company's artificial-intelligence spending plans, they write, AI is having a meaningful impact on revenue growth. "While we acknowledge the investor concern that Meta lacks a public cloud (and thus revenue is more tied to Meta specific products), we believe this is ignoring the solid returns in the core today," they write. The company will need to release more AI products to improve investor sentiment, they write, saying that Meta is at the "show me" phase of the AI development cycle. Shares are down 9.2% to $607.74. (elias.schisgall@wsj.com)

data.title: RBI shifts 104 tonnes of gold to domestic vaults, raises share in forex reserves
data.description:
The Reserve Bank of India has moved another 104.23 metric tonnes of its gold holdings domestically in the six months ended March 2026, according to its half-yearly report on foreign exchange reserves., news agency PTI reported. The central bank’s overall gold holdings rose marginally to 880.52 MT in March 2026 from 880.18 MT at the end of September 2025.

The report said the quantum of gold held domestically stood at 290.37 MT in March 2026, compared to 575.82 MT in September last year and 511.99 MT in March 2025. It added that 197.67 MT of gold was kept in safe custody with the Bank of England and the Bank for International Settlements (BIS), while 2.80 MT was held in the form of gold deposits as of March 2026.

In September, the central bank had disclosed that 290.37 MT of gold was held with the Bank of England and BIS, while 13.99 MT was in the form of gold deposits. The rally in precious metal prices pushed the share of gold in India’s forex reserves to 16.7 per cent in March from 13.92 per cent six months earlier.

Out of the total foreign currency assets of USD 552.28 billion, USD 465.61 billion was invested in securities, USD 46.83 billion was deposited with other central banks and the BIS, and the remaining USD 39.84 billion comprised deposits with commercial banks overseas.

There were minor changes in the deployment pattern of foreign currency assets, with investments in securities and deposits with commercial banks overseas declining slightly in favour of deposits with other central banks and the BIS.

The Reserve Bank’s net forward assets (payable) stood at USD 103.06 billion at the end of March 2026, the report said.

*With Agency Inputs

data.title: Rolls-Royce Holds Guidance As Widebody Engine Recovery Gains Traction
data.description:
Rolls-Royce (RYCEY) is giving investors a potentially important resilience signal at a difficult moment for global aviation. The Iran war has pushed jet fuel prices higher, disrupted airspace and raised concerns over drone attacks on infrastructure, yet Rolls-Royce said in a Thursday trading update that airline usage of its widebody engines has seen a significant recovery. The company also reiterated its earnings targets for the year, suggesting that the pressure hitting parts of the airline industry has not yet derailed its own financial outlook. For investors, the central detail is where the capacity cuts are showing up: Rolls-Royce said the vast majority of economically driven airline capacity reductions announced year-to-date have been in narrowbody aircraft, a segment where the company does not operate.

That could matter because Rolls-Royce's commercial aviation exposure is tied more closely to widebody aircraft, including Airbus SE's A350 powered by its Trent XWB engines, rather than short-haul aircraft such as the Airbus A320 or Boeing Co.'s 737. Rolls-Royce said usage of Trent XWB engines in the region has returned to pre-war levels, suggesting Emirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad Airways have resumed operating their most advanced airliner. The broader Gulf airline recovery is not complete, with carriers having canceled thousands of flights and not yet fully restored operations, but Rolls-Royce said it is proactively mitigating the impact linked to the Middle East conflict. That framing could help investors separate industry-wide airline stress from Rolls-Royce's own widebody engine position.

The market response pointed to some relief, with Rolls-Royce rising as much as 2.7% in London trading, even though the shares are little changed so far this year after more than doubling in value in 2025. Management maintained guidance for underlying operating profit of as much as 4.2 billion and free cash flow of 3.6 billion to 3.8 billion for the year. The update does not remove the risks tied to the Iran conflict, fuel prices or airspace disruption, but it does suggest Rolls-Royce may be better insulated than airlines and suppliers tied more heavily to narrowbody flying. For investors watching whether Middle East disruption could interrupt the company's recovery story, the message from Rolls-Royce was cautious but constructive: widebody engine usage is recovering, guidance remains intact, and the biggest capacity cuts appear to be happening outside its core aircraft market.

data.title: How SoftBank Can Give Tesla Stock a Boost — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Al Root

The robots are coming. And a new robot company is coming for investors.

Wednesday evening, the Financial Times reported that SoftBank was going to list an AI robotics company in the U.S., valued at up to $100 billion.

SoftBank didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

The company, called Roze, likely includes assets, including ABB's robotics division, that SoftBank agreed to buy in October for $5.4 billion.

Those are industrial robots, which don't walk out of the factory. All the buzz recently about AI-trained robots relates to humanoids that can move freely in their environment and learn to do tasks at human levels of proficiency, thanks to AI computing.

Investors will have to see what other assets are part of Roze. SoftBank reportedly filed confidential documents with the Securities and Exchange Commission. Confidential filings aren't a surprise. They have become a typical way companies start their IPO journey. Eventually, filings will be made public, usually several weeks before the IPO.

The IPO will be an interesting valuation mark for robots. U.S. investors don't have many pure-play, AI-trained robotics companies. Tesla is spending billions to build robotics manufacturing capacity, but Tesla is also an EV, robo-taxi, and energy storage company.

Valuing robots is hard. There are essentially no useful humanoid robots working in the economy now, but Wall Street sees trillions in potential revenue down the road. Addressable markets are typically based on global labor markets, which are measured in the tens of trillions of dollars annually.

Wall Street's Tesla robot valuations are all over the place. RBC analyst Tom Narayan projects some $400 billion in Tesla robot sales in 2050, and values that at 10 times sales. Discounting that back works out to some $640 billion today. Deutsche Bank analyst Edison Yu doesn't look out to 2050. He projects 1.25 million robots sold in 2035 for about $25,000 each. That's $31 billion in revenue. He puts a 30-times multiple on that revenue, calling robots worth about $111 per share today. That works out to a $500 billion valuation based on Tesla's fully diluted share count. Morgan Stanley values Tesla's robots at closer to $270 billion.

Hyundai Motor's Boston Dynamics, on the other hand, is valued between $20 billion and $30 billion. That's a low number, relative to the others, for a company with the impressive humanoid robot, Atlas.

Whatever the numbers, a successful Roze listing could give the entire sector a boost.

Eventually, of course, robots will have to show up and do useful work to justify any of the billion-dollar valuations.

The bottleneck to robot growth might not be the brains but the brawn. AI computing is good enough to create useful robots. Right now, the manufacturing capacity and supply chain to support millions of robots simply doesn't exist.

That still needs to be built, and building out a trillion-dollar industry will take time.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Union Pacific, Norfolk Southern Refile Rail-Merger Application — WSJ
data.description:
By Esther Fung

The proposed $71.5 billion merger between Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern to form a coast-to-coast railroad run by a single operator will have a projected 39% of the market share of rail freight in the U.S., the two railroads said.

The companies on Thursday resubmitted their merger application to U.S. regulators, this time including a description of conditions that would compel Union Pacific to walk away from the merger. Such conditions typically include requirements that the buyers cede control of certain parts of the railroad and concessions that allow other rail carriers access to its rail lines and facilities.

In January, the Surface Transportation Board rejected the railroads' initial application, saying that it was incomplete, and invited the railroads to revise their application. Market-share projections and a list of conditions that would cause Union Pacific to walk away were among the items that the regulator had asked for when it asked the two companies to re-apply. Some of the information is confidential and redacted in the public filing.

The Surface Transportation Board is overseeing the lengthy merger-review process and will decide to approve or reject the merger. If the board accepts the latest application, it will review public comment and rebuttals in the coming months before making a decision sometime in 2027.

The proposed deal already has drawn skepticism from several lawmakers, state attorneys general and rail customers. They have asked the regulator to scrutinize the transaction, asserting that the combined company could concentrate market power, stifle competition and lead to higher prices and poorer service. Other railroads have lined up against it, saying that the rail industry is consolidated enough.

The two railroads, which control tracks on opposite sides of the Mississippi River, said that the merger merits include reducing bottlenecks at the current interchange terminals which would speed up long-haul deliveries to retailers and factories. The revised application provided a new estimate that the merger will lead to $3.5 billion in savings to shippers, based on a projection that shippers won't have to use as many trucks. The applicants said the merged railroad will take 2.1 million trucks off the roads.

"This merger enhances competition and delivers real public benefits," said Jim Vena, chief executive officer of Union Pacific.

The applicants added that they will add a new service connecting Northern California to the Southeast, and that the overall expected increase in business will drive 1,200 new union jobs over three years.

After a series of railroad mergers over the past several decades, a duopoly effectively operates east of the Mississippi in Norfolk Southern and CSX, and a duopoly exists west of the Mississippi in BNSF Railway and Union Pacific. Railroad analysts and investors have said that if Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern were allowed to merge, BNSF could be compelled to merge with CSX as well.

"This did not begin with a customer asking for a UP-NS merger to happen, " said the president and chief executive officer of BNSF, Katie Farmer. "It's driven by Wall Street on the promise of a big shareholder payout. It will eliminate competition, raise costs for consumers, and destabilize the supply chain that powers the American economy."

The attorneys general of Montana, Tennessee, Kansas, North Dakota, South Dakota, Mississippi and Iowa have voiced opposition to the merger, adding that the deal would "shackle domestic manufacturers, energy producers, and farmers with higher rates and fewer shipping options, thereby blunting their competitive edge against foreign rivals, squeezing household budgets, and weakening the supply chains that underpin our national security."

Write to Esther Fung at esther.fung@wsj.com
